video_title: One of the World's Largest Hedge Funds on its 86x Growth in Token Spending | Odd Lots
gist: Man Group CTO Gary Collier and Head of Data & AI Tushar Fernando describe an 86x rise in the hedge fund's internal AI token consumption since January, driven by firm-wide agentic workflows that already span discretionary research synthesis and an 18-month-old multi-agent pipeline that has taken 15-20 systematic strategies from AI-generated hypothesis to live client capital. They argue the durable edge lies in proprietary data structuring and market-access infrastructure, not in access to frontier models themselves.

themes.theme: Token Consumption Explosion & Firm-Wide AI Adoption

insights.claim: Man Group's internal AI token consumption has increased 86-fold since January 2026, growing across every department including finance, operations and HR, not just technology teams.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: A near-90x compute/token spend increase within roughly six months signals explosive enterprise AI adoption and foreshadows a coming inflection where firms must justify token spend against measurable ROI (cost savings or revenue).
insights.quote: since January, I think I was, token consumption has gone up 86 times

insights.claim: Even after 86x growth in token usage, Man Group has not yet needed to impose token austerity or build a router to force cheaper models, implying meaningful headroom remains before AI compute costs become a binding constraint for large asset managers.
insights.speaker: host, Odd Lots (Joe Weisenthal)
insights.so_what: If a sophisticated firm can absorb 86x usage growth without cost discipline becoming urgent, bearish narratives about AI token costs crowding out budgets may be premature — a mildly bullish read-through for AI compute/infrastructure demand.
insights.quote: kind of bullish if you think about it, for token spend that you can grow 86 eggs and it still doesn't get you to the point where like, oh, we got to really like clamp down on this

insights.claim: Man Group's AI token spend is increasingly driven by autonomous agents executing workflows rather than individual human queries, creating an unresolved question of which business unit owns a given workflow and its cost.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: As agentic spend scales, cost attribution and internal chargeback will become a real governance challenge for asset managers deploying AI at scale — a gap enterprise tooling vendors could target.
insights.quote: the spend is actually not by people as by agents, and the agents relate to workflows

themes.theme: AI as Research Augmentation, Not a Trading Signal Generator

insights.claim: Simplistic uses of AI such as asking a chatbot which stock to buy or what inflation will be produce no investable edge; the institutional value of AI in investing instead comes from synthesizing and filtering large volumes of unstructured research inputs (earnings calls, broker research, alternative data, podcasts) for portfolio managers.
insights.speaker: host, Odd Lots
insights.so_what: Investors should be skeptical of retail-style 'AI stock picker' products and instead look for AI value in research-workflow efficiency at established asset managers.

insights.claim: At Man Group, discretionary portfolio managers have AI agents asynchronously scan and synthesize earnings reports, broker research, alternative data and podcasts overnight, surfacing only the insights relevant to a specific position while the PM is away from their desk.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: This async overnight-research-synthesis pattern is a concrete, adoptable workflow other active managers could replicate to compress research cycle time without adding headcount.
insights.quote: the agent has actually distilled a change that's happened on the internet. And given them the insight that's meaningful to their portfolio, to their investment thesis.

insights.claim: A Man Group portfolio manager covering the AI trade had an agent transcribe and synthesize a podcast featuring a hyperscaler's head of engineering, surfacing that GPU scarcity and insufficient large-scale data-center capacity — plus a coming need for better inter-datacenter networking — are near-term bottlenecks to AI model training, an insight the PM would not otherwise have accessed since such engineering leaders don't appear on investor calls.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: Flags GPU supply and data-center capacity/networking as a specific, near-term supply-chain constraint on the AI trade, sourced from primary engineering commentary rather than IR messaging.
insights.quote: it was increasingly important to have more and more GPUs to train models, but data centers were becoming more scarce, and it was becoming increasingly difficult to find data centers that were actually big enough to train these models

insights.claim: Podcasts featuring operational or engineering leaders, rather than only executives on investor calls, are becoming a distinct alternative-data source for institutional investors now that AI transcription and synthesis tools make it economical to mine long-form audio at scale.
insights.speaker: host, Odd Lots
insights.so_what: Watch for asset managers building or buying podcast-transcription-and-synthesis tooling as a new alternative-data category.
insights.quote: I feel like podcasts are an important source of AlphaGo.

themes.theme: Agentic Pipeline for Systematic Strategy Research

insights.claim: Man Group has spent roughly a year and a half building a multi-agent pipeline that automates the full quant research lifecycle: agents read academic papers and labeled datasets to form economic hypotheses, other agents write code to test them, pull market data and run backtests, and further agents evaluate the outputs of prior agents.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: A working, near-production example of 'agentic alpha discovery' — a template for how systematic shops could industrialize idea generation itself, not just idea execution.
insights.quote: the idea formation part, looking at academic papers, looking at carefully labeled, data sets, reasoning about the contents of those papers, the contents of those data sets, are there economic hypotheses

insights.claim: As of mid-2026, roughly 15-20 AI-originated systematic trading models at Man Group have gone from an initial agent-generated idea through human investment-committee review and validation to live deployment of client assets.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that agent-originated trading strategies are already in live production at a major hedge fund, not just a research demo — a leading indicator for how fast AI-generated alpha could scale industry-wide.
insights.quote: The validation process were reviewed and validated by a human investment committee and things like fit and proper to triage our client's assets.

insights.claim: Before an AI agent at Man Group writes any code for a new trading strategy, it must first produce a plain-English investment hypothesis and rationale, which human reviewers actually evaluate — explainability is built into the front of the pipeline rather than bolted on afterward.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: A regulated-industry blueprint for auditable agentic research: require a mandatory natural-language rationale gate before any code or execution step.
insights.quote: one of the first things it does before it moves on to writing, codes, it's giving us a real English description of, what it thinks. The rationale for the trading signal is.

insights.claim: Man Group's trading and holding periods run days to months rather than microseconds, and the firm deliberately stays out of uninterpretable high-frequency, deep-neural-net signal spaces so every trading decision remains explainable to risk committees and regulators.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: Firms adopting AI-driven alpha in longer-holding-period strategies face a real design choice between raw model performance and auditability — Man Group is choosing auditability by construction, which could be a competitive constraint or a regulatory moat depending on how AI oversight rules evolve.
insights.quote: We never want to be in a position where we don't know why that trade happened.

themes.theme: Data Architecture: Structuring, Tagging and the Semantic Layer

insights.claim: Man Group ingests roughly a terabyte of raw exchange tick-by-tick data per day, on top of unstructured alternative data and a newer 'institutional knowledge' layer that captures internal process context for AI systems.
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of structured market-data infrastructure incumbents already have — an advantage new AI-native entrants would need years to replicate.
insights.quote: we ingest every take from most exchanges, almost a terabyte of data just from takes per day

insights.claim: The highest-ROI part of Man Group's AI data pipeline is not model fine-tuning but pre-processing: adding plain-English descriptive metadata to raw datasets (e.g., annotating that each row of credit-card data represents a specific consumer purchase) so AI systems understand data semantics without hallucinating context.
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: Suggests enterprise AI capital is better spent on data annotation and semantic infrastructure than on model fine-tuning — a contrarian allocation call relevant to how firms should budget AI investment.
insights.quote: what we're having to do is invest in trying to add extra color, extra metadata to that information. We want to have descriptors to tell it, the nuances.

insights.claim: Man Group built a unified semantic tagging layer across all its datasets so AI systems can automatically navigate between related fields — for example linking a ticker in one dataset to sector data in another — which the firm treats as a prerequisite for cross-dataset idea generation.
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: A concrete architectural pattern (a unified tagging/semantic layer) other data-heavy investment firms could benchmark against when building AI research infrastructure.
insights.quote: investing in a shared language, a shared semantic layer or a shared way of doing things is something that we've had to do so that we tag all of the columns with this unified language

insights.claim: For coding tasks the newest frontier LLM matters most, but for quant research the quality of underlying proprietary/structured data — not model choice — is what drives alpha; pointing a frontier model at a research problem without that data foundation 'will get you nowhere.'
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: Reinforces that data and infrastructure moats, not model access, are the durable edge in AI-driven investing — relevant to how investors should value AI-adopting asset managers versus pure model providers.
insights.quote: for a quant research task, I think the looking for the underlying data is what fuels alpha research. Trying to just use a frontier model will get you nowhere.

insights.claim: A Bridgewater research paper found that fine-tuning an open-source model referred to in this conversation as 'Quinn' (likely Alibaba's Qwen) on proprietary data to classify newsworthy financial information outperformed the most advanced frontier US models at that specific task.
insights.speaker: host, Odd Lots
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that proprietary-data fine-tuning of an open-weight model can beat frontier closed models on a narrow financial-classification task, supporting the broader thesis that data — not raw model capability — is where edge concentrates.
insights.quote: They found that the combination of open source models plus proprietary data got them better results than the most frontier US models.

themes.theme: Token Economics, Model Governance and Efficiency

insights.claim: Man Group deliberately does not build an automatic classifier or router to steer every query to the cheapest adequate model, instead relying on transparent budget reporting and education so its roughly 1,700-1,800 staff learn firsthand which model tier fits which task.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: A deliberate anti-automation choice in cost governance, betting that firm-wide AI literacy is worth more than short-term token savings from routing — relevant to how enterprises should design AI cost-control tooling.
insights.quote: We we could do that. We've chosen not to. I think that the reason is that we want people to understand the dynamics of how best to use AI, and which models to use.

insights.claim: A common token-wasting mistake among Man Group employees was mixing unrelated tasks — drafting an investment thesis, planning lunch, checking tomorrow's weather — inside the same context window with a frontier model.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: A concrete, generalizable failure mode (context-window mixing of unrelated tasks) any enterprise rolling out AI broadly should train against to control cost.
insights.quote: often people would be doing multiple tasks in the same context to window. They'd be trying to figure out the best way to write an investment thesis, and they were trying to figure out where to go to lunch.

insights.claim: Man Group employees found grassroots ways to cut token spend, such as intercepting simple, deterministic tool calls (e.g., basic git commands inside a coding agent) so the full raw output isn't fed back through the LLM for processing, then contributing those savings techniques back to the shared AI platform.
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: A specific, transferable engineering pattern — intercept deterministic tool calls outside the agent loop — for cutting LLM token costs in coding-agent workflows.
insights.quote: there's some really simple tools, some simple basic steps that you could use that instead of calling an LLN to do inference and tool calls, it can just intercept that tool call and do it outside of the agenda loop

insights.claim: AI budget allocation at Man Group is federated down to individual business units with fungible budgets, letting departments reallocate their own spend toward more token usage if they choose, rather than being centrally rationed.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete organizational template — federated, fungible AI budgets — for large enterprises balancing central cost control against ground-level agility.
insights.quote: budgets are fungible. If departments wants to move money from some other spend and say, right, I think we should buy more tokens and they are free to, to do that.

insights.claim: Citing a benchmark referred to in the conversation as 'meta' (likely a reference to, or mishearing of, the METR AI-task-horizon benchmark), the length of task an AI agent can autonomously complete has been doubling roughly every seven months, meaning agents can now be tasked with work that would take a human around 16 hours.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: A quantifiable growth curve for agent capability (task-length doubling roughly every 7 months) investors can use to model how quickly agentic labor substitution could scale across knowledge-work sectors, including asset management itself.
insights.quote: every seven months or so, the amount of time, and the genetic workflow can go away and do a task is doubling. So now you can ask an agent to do a task that would take a human 16 hours.

themes.theme: Talent, Organization and the Future of Investment Labor

insights.claim: Man Group now expects every new hire, regardless of role — including operations staff, not just front-office or technology roles — to meet a baseline level of AI fluency as a hiring bar.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: Signals that AI fluency is becoming a universal hiring filter at sophisticated asset managers, not just for technologists — relevant to how finance labor markets will re-price skills.
insights.quote: everyone we hire now into the firm should up the bar with respect to AI, regardless of the role that they're coming in today

insights.claim: The most valuable technologists at Man Group are shifting from hands-on 'in the weeds' debugging toward acting as high-level orchestrators who plan and direct multiple AI agents end-to-end, a mindset shift many engineers find personally difficult because they prefer working in the details.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: Points to a re-skilling requirement within technology teams themselves — orchestration and strategic planning over hands-on coding — relevant to how tech talent should be evaluated and compensated going forward.
insights.quote: You want to be a kind of a conductor of these agents. You want to be in charge of the end-to-end process rather than necessarily being in the weeds.

insights.claim: Man Group has run an internal 'forward deployed engineer' model — technologists embedded directly with quants and discretionary managers — for roughly 15 years, predating the current industry wave around Microsoft's 'Frontier Company' initiative, Palantir's forward-deployed-engineer approach, and Anthropic's practice of embedding engineers at clients like Goldman Sachs.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: Undercuts the framing that 'forward-deployed engineering' is a novel AI-era innovation — buy-side firms with mature internal platform teams may already have a structural head start over new entrants selling org-design consulting for AI adoption.
insights.quote: that's the way we've been, like set up internally here for about, I think 15 years

insights.claim: Man Group sees both 'superstar' employees who become expert orchestrators of many AI agents across teams and a broader democratization effect where junior staff automate tasks and learn faster via AI, with the firm currently observing more of the latter given its size, even as the smaller number of superstar users increasingly work across multiple teams.
insights.speaker: guest, Man Group
insights.so_what: Suggests the net labor-market effect of enterprise AI adoption at a large organization currently favors broad skill democratization over concentration in a few superstars — relevant to debates about AI's effect on wage and skill dispersion.

themes.theme: Alpha Durability, Market Access and Knowledge Alignment

insights.claim: As AI makes it easy for anyone to onboard and analyze new datasets, those datasets are becoming 'table stakes' or risk factors rather than proprietary alpha sources; durable edge instead comes from combining decades-old market access, broker relationships and execution infrastructure with the data.
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: A framework for distinguishing AI-enabled but commoditizable data edges from durable structural edges (market access, execution relationships) — useful for assessing which AI-driven trading strategies are likely to get arbitraged away quickly.
insights.quote: some features of data sets will become table stakes. They go from being alpha to being a risk factor. But because everybody has them and it moves the market. But it isn't the only way that we make money.

insights.claim: AI is incrementally expanding the set of tradeable markets by lowering the human labor cost of establishing the two prerequisites for a systematic strategy — reliable market connectivity and the ability to determine an instrument's price — opening systematic access to historically hard-to-price markets like crypto and securitized credit sooner than would otherwise be possible.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, less-obvious AI-driven expansion of the systematic-trading opportunity set into historically under-covered or hard-to-price markets, worth watching for early-mover positioning.

insights.claim: A Man Group team built a relatively simple AI system that reads complex financial instruments' PDF specification documents and automatically populates a reference-data store, illustrating the kind of small, incremental automation accumulating firm-wide to expand coverage of previously labor-constrained markets.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete, low-glamour but replicable automation use case that materially cuts the cost of covering niche or complex instruments.

insights.claim: In Man Group's highly collaborative systematic/quant business, most workflow knowledge is already shared via common code and playbooks, but the remaining idiosyncratic 'secret sauce' tied to a discretionary investor's personal edge or compensation is something staff are notably more reluctant to feed into shared AI knowledge systems.
insights.speaker: Gary Collier, Man Group CTO
insights.so_what: Flags an unresolved alignment tension in enterprise AI knowledge capture: the highest-value, most idiosyncratic expertise is exactly what star performers are least incentivized to hand over to a shared AI system.
insights.quote: there's the 10%. Well, you know, this is where my personal value lies. I'm not so comfortable talking about, about that.

insights.claim: Man Group has encoded the shared, non-proprietary parts of the investment workflow — such as how to run a backtest or how to structure reading an investment report — into reusable 'AI playbooks' and 'AI skills' stored on an internal knowledge platform, deliberately kept separate from the strategy-specific secret sauce that stays with individual investors.
insights.speaker: Tushar Fernando, Man Group Head of Data & AI
insights.so_what: A concrete organizational pattern for capturing institutional process knowledge in AI-usable form while preserving individual incentive structures — a template other firms could copy.
insights.quote: those areas are encapsulated in AI playbooks, AI skills that we put in our knowledge platform. And they can be used across the floor.
